D: Original: Crito - Plato (Benjamin Jowett translation)
D: Narrative: Crito - Plato - Narrative
D: Refashioned: Crito - Plato - Refashioned

D: Original:
SOCRATES: Why have you come at this hour, Crito? it must be quite early?

CRITO: Yes, certainly.

SOCRATES: What is the exact time?

CRITO: The dawn is breaking.

SOCRATES: I wonder that the keeper of the prison would let you in.

CRITO: He knows me, because I often come, Socrates; moreover, I have done him a kindness.

SOCRATES: And are you only just arrived?

CRITO: No, I came some time ago.

SOCRATES: Then why did you sit and say nothing, instead of at once awakening me?

CRITO: I should not have liked myself, Socrates, to be in such great trouble and unrest as you are—indeed I should not: I have been watching with amazement your peaceful slumbers; and for that reason I did not awake you, because I wished to minimize the pain. I have always thought you to be of a happy disposition; but never did I see anything like the easy, tranquil manner in which you bear this calamity.

SOCRATES: Why, Crito, when a man has reached my age he ought not to be repining at the approach of death.

CRITO: And yet other old men find themselves in similar misfortunes, and age does not prevent them from repining.

SOCRATES: That is true. But you have not told me why you come at this early hour.

CRITO: I come to bring you a message which is sad and painful; not, as I believe, to yourself, but to all of us who are your friends, and saddest of all to me.

SOCRATES: What? Has the ship come from Delos, on the arrival of which I am to die?

CRITO: No, the ship has not actually arrived, but she will probably be here to-day, as persons who have come from Sunium tell me that they have left her there; and therefore to-morrow, Socrates, will be the last day of your life.

SOCRATES: Very well, Crito; if such is the will of God, I am willing; but my belief is that there will be a delay of a day.

CRITO: Why do you think so?

SOCRATES: I will tell you. I am to die on the day after the arrival of the ship?

CRITO: Yes; that is what the authorities say.
D: Narrative:
SOCRATES: Why have you come so early, Crito? It must be very early in the morning.

CRITO: Yes, it certainly is.

SOCRATES: What time is it exactly?

CRITO: Dawn is just breaking.

SOCRATES: I'm surprised the prison guard let you in at this hour.

CRITO: He knows me well because I visit often, Socrates. Besides, I've done him some favors.

SOCRATES: Did you just get here?

CRITO: No, I arrived a while ago.

SOCRATES: Then why did you just sit there in silence instead of waking me right away?

CRITO: I wouldn't have wanted to, Socrates. I wouldn't want to be in such distress and sleeplessness as you must be. I've been sitting here watching you sleep so peacefully, and that's exactly why I didn't wake you—I wanted to spare you as much suffering as possible. I've always admired your even temperament, but never more than now, seeing the calm and easy way you bear this disaster.

SOCRATES: Well, Crito, it would be foolish for a man my age to be upset that death is approaching.

CRITO: And yet other old men in similar situations find that age doesn't stop them from being distressed about it.

SOCRATES: That's true enough. But you still haven't told me why you've come so early.

CRITO: I bring you news that is sad and painful—not for you, I think, but for all of us who are your friends, and most painful of all for me.

SOCRATES: What is it? Has the ship arrived from Delos—the one whose return means I must die?

CRITO: No, the ship hasn't actually arrived yet, but it will probably get here today. People coming from Sunium say they saw it there. So tomorrow, Socrates, will be the last day of your life.

SOCRATES: Very well, Crito. If that is the will of God, so be it. But I believe there will be a delay of one more day.

CRITO: Why do you think so?

SOCRATES: I'll explain. I'm to die on the day after the ship arrives, correct?

CRITO: Yes, that's what the authorities say.
D: Refashioned:
SOCRATES: Crito, why are you here so early? It can't even be sunrise yet.

CRITO: It is, just barely.

SOCRATES: What time is it?

CRITO: Dawn is breaking.

SOCRATES: I'm surprised the guard let you in.

CRITO: He knows me—I come here often, and I've been generous with him.

SOCRATES: Have you only just arrived?

CRITO: No, I've been here a while.

SOCRATES: Then why didn't you wake me instead of sitting there saying nothing?

CRITO: Because I couldn't bring myself to do it. Honestly, Socrates, I wouldn't want to be in your position—facing what you're facing—and yet here you are, sleeping peacefully. I've been watching you in amazement. I've always known you to be remarkably composed, but I've never seen anything like the calm way you're handling all of this.

SOCRATES: Crito, when a man reaches my age, he really shouldn't be afraid of dying.

CRITO: But plenty of old men in your situation would disagree—age doesn't cure people of the fear of death.

SOCRATES: True enough. But what brings you here at this hour?

CRITO: I have painful news. Not painful for you, I suspect, but devastating for your friends—and worst of all for me.

SOCRATES: What news? Has the sacred ship from Delos arrived? Its return is my death signal.

CRITO: Not quite—it hasn't docked yet, but travelers from Sunium report it was there when they left. It will almost certainly arrive today, which means tomorrow, Socrates, will be your last day alive.

SOCRATES: Then so be it, if God wills it. But I believe we have one more day than you think.

CRITO: What makes you say that?

SOCRATES: Let me explain. I'm to die the day after the ship arrives, right?

CRITO: That's what the authorities have said, yes.
D: Elegant: Crito arrives at Socrates' prison cell before dawn, having sat watching his friend sleep in remarkable tranquility. He brings grim news: the sacred ship from Delos will likely arrive today, meaning Socrates will be executed tomorrow. Socrates receives this calmly, noting that a man his age should not fear death, and suggests from a dream that they may have one more day than expected.
D: Summary: Crito visits Socrates in prison before dawn and reports that the sacred ship from Delos is expected to arrive today, which would set Socrates' execution for the following day. Socrates receives the news with striking composure and suggests there may be a one-day delay.
D: Distilled: Crito brings news of Socrates' imminent execution; Socrates receives it with calm equanimity.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates, Crito. The sacred ship from Delos refers to the annual Athenian mission commemorating Theseus' voyage to Crete; no executions could be carried out from the time it departed until it returned, which delayed Socrates' death by about a month after his trial. Crito was a wealthy Athenian of the same deme (Alopece) as Socrates and one of his closest lifelong friends. Socrates' calm in the face of death here prefigures the famous death scene in the Phaedo, and his remark about old age and death echoes themes from the Republic's opening conversation with Cephalus.

D: Original:
SOCRATES: But I do not think that the ship will be here until to-morrow; this I infer from a vision which I had last night, or rather only just now, when you fortunately allowed me to sleep.

CRITO: And what was the nature of the vision?

SOCRATES: There appeared to me the likeness of a woman, fair and comely, clothed in bright raiment, who called to me and said: O Socrates,

‘The third day hence to fertile Phthia shalt thou go.’ 3

CRITO: What a singular dream, Socrates!

SOCRATES: There can be no doubt about the meaning, Crito, I think.
D: Narrative:
SOCRATES: But I don't think the ship will arrive until tomorrow. I'm basing this on a dream I had last night—or really just now, since you were kind enough to let me go on sleeping.

CRITO: What was the dream?

SOCRATES: I saw what appeared to be a beautiful woman, dressed in white, who came to me and said: "Socrates, on the third day you shall reach the fertile land of Phthia."

CRITO: What a strange dream, Socrates!

SOCRATES: But its meaning seems clear enough, I think, Crito.
D: Refashioned:
SOCRATES: I don't think the ship will get here until tomorrow. I say this because of a vision I had just now, while you kindly let me sleep.

CRITO: What kind of vision?

SOCRATES: A beautiful woman appeared to me, dressed in radiant white, and she called out: "Socrates, on the third day you shall arrive in fertile Phthia."

CRITO: What a peculiar dream!

SOCRATES: And yet its meaning is quite clear, don't you think?
D: Elegant: Socrates recounts a dream in which a beautiful woman told him he would reach "fertile Phthia" on the third day—echoing Achilles' words in the Iliad about returning home. He interprets this as meaning his death (his soul's homecoming) is three days away, not two, and that the ship will be delayed by a day.
D: Summary: Socrates describes a prophetic dream in which a woman tells him he will reach Phthia on the third day, which he interprets as meaning the ship from Delos will arrive tomorrow and his execution will follow the day after.
D: Distilled: Socrates interprets a dream quoting Homer as a sign that his death is three days away, not two.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates, Crito. The quote "on the third day you shall reach fertile Phthia" adapts Achilles' words from Homer's Iliad (9.363), where Achilles threatens to leave Troy and sail home to Phthia in three days. Socrates reinterprets "Phthia" as the afterlife—his soul's true home. Socrates frequently reported receiving divine signs and prophetic dreams; this connects to the broader Greek tradition of significant dreams before death, and to his famous daimonion (divine sign) mentioned in the Apology.

D: Original: CRITO: Yes; the meaning is only too clear. But, oh! my beloved Socrates, let me entreat you once more to take my advice and escape. For if you die I shall not only lose a friend who can never be replaced, but there is another evil: people who do not know you and me will believe that I might have saved you if I had been willing to give money, but that I did not care. Now, can there be a worse disgrace than this—that I should be thought to value money more than the life of a friend? For the many will not be persuaded that I wanted you to escape, and that you refused.
D: Narrative: CRITO: Yes, the meaning is all too clear. But please, my dear Socrates, I'm begging you one more time: take my advice and escape. If you die, it won't just mean losing a friend who can never be replaced. There's something else that troubles me: people who don't know us will believe that I could have saved you if I'd been willing to spend the money, but that I simply didn't care enough. And what could be more disgraceful than being thought to value money over the life of a friend? Most people will never believe that it was you who refused to escape and that I tried to convince you.
D: Refashioned: CRITO: Yes, the meaning is painfully clear. But Socrates, I'm pleading with you again—let me help you escape. If you die, I don't just lose an irreplaceable friend. I also face a terrible reputation. People who don't really know either of us will assume I had the means to save you but chose not to—that I cared more about my money than about your life. Can you imagine a worse disgrace? No matter what really happened, the public will never accept that you chose to stay and that I begged you to go.
D: Elegant: Crito makes an emotional appeal: Socrates' death means the irreplaceable loss of a friend, and worse, the public will assume Crito valued money over Socrates' life. No one will believe Socrates refused to escape of his own accord.
D: Summary: Crito pleads with Socrates to escape, arguing that he will lose an irreplaceable friend and that the public will blame Crito for valuing money over Socrates' life, since no one will believe Socrates chose to stay.
D: Distilled: Crito begs Socrates to escape, fearing both personal loss and public disgrace if people think he failed to save his friend.
Notes: Speaker: Crito. This passage reveals Crito's dual motivation: genuine love for Socrates and concern for his own public reputation. His worry about what "the many" will think sets up Socrates' key philosophical response about the opinion of the majority versus the opinion of the wise—a central theme of the dialogue. Crito's emphasis on wealth and reputation reflects conventional Athenian aristocratic values, which Socrates will methodically challenge.

D: Original:
SOCRATES: But why, my dear Crito, should we care about the opinion of the many? Good men, and they are the only persons who are worth considering, will think of these things truly as they occurred.

CRITO: But you see, Socrates, that the opinion of the many must be regarded, for what is now happening shows that they can do the greatest evil to anyone who has lost their good opinion.

SOCRATES: I only wish it were so, Crito; and that the many could do the greatest evil; for then they would also be able to do the greatest good—and what a fine thing this would be! But in reality they can do neither; for they cannot make a man either wise or foolish; and whatever they do is the result of chance.
D: Narrative:
SOCRATES: But why, my dear Crito, should we care about what most people think? The good and reasonable people—the only ones worth considering—will understand the truth of what actually happened.

CRITO: But you can see, Socrates, that we do have to take the opinion of the majority seriously. What's happening to you right now proves that the crowd is perfectly capable of inflicting the greatest harm on anyone who falls out of their favor.

SOCRATES: I only wish that were true, Crito—that the majority could do the greatest harm. Because then they would also be capable of doing the greatest good, and that would be a wonderful thing! But the reality is that they can do neither. They can't make a person wise or foolish. Whatever they do is essentially random.
D: Refashioned:
SOCRATES: But Crito, why should we worry about what ordinary people think? The only opinions that matter belong to good, thoughtful people—and they will see the situation for what it really is.

CRITO: That's easy to say, Socrates, but look at your situation! Public opinion has put you here. The majority clearly has the power to do terrible things to anyone who loses their approval.

SOCRATES: I wish the majority really did have that power, Crito. Because if they could do the greatest evil, they could also do the greatest good—and wouldn't that be something? But in truth, they can do neither. They can't make anyone wise, and they can't make anyone foolish. They just act without real understanding, and the results are essentially a matter of chance.
D: Elegant: Socrates dismisses concern about popular opinion: only the judgment of wise and good people matters. When Crito points out that the majority has the power to kill, Socrates counters that true power would require the ability to make people wise or foolish—which the crowd cannot do.
D: Summary: Socrates argues that only the opinions of wise people matter, not the majority's. When Crito objects that the crowd clearly has the power to harm, Socrates replies that since the majority cannot make anyone wise or foolish, their power is ultimately superficial.
D: Distilled: Socrates argues the majority's opinion is worthless because they lack the power to make people truly better or worse.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates, Crito. This exchange introduces the dialogue's central philosophical theme: the distinction between expert opinion and popular opinion. Socrates' claim that the many "can do neither the greatest evil nor the greatest good" connects to the Gorgias, where he argues that tyrants have no real power because they cannot achieve what is truly good. The argument also foreshadows the extended analogy (in Part 2) between the expert trainer of the body and the expert in matters of justice for the soul.

D: Original:
CRITO: Well, I will not dispute with you; but please to tell me, Socrates, whether you are not acting out of regard to me and your other friends: are you not afraid that if you escape from prison we may get into trouble with the informers for having stolen you away, and lose either the whole or a great part of our property; or that even a worse evil may happen to us? Now, if you fear on our account, be at ease; for in order to save you, we ought surely to run this, or even a greater risk; be persuaded, then, and do as I say.

SOCRATES: Yes, Crito, that is one fear which you mention, but by no means the only one.
D: Narrative:
CRITO: Well, I won't argue that point. But tell me, Socrates—are you holding back out of concern for me and your other friends? Are you worried that if you escape, the informers will come after us for helping you, and we'll lose our property or suffer something even worse? If that's what you're afraid of, put it out of your mind. We should absolutely be willing to take that risk to save you—or even a greater one. Please, let me persuade you. Do as I say.

SOCRATES: Yes, Crito, that is one of my concerns, but it's far from the only one.
D: Refashioned:
CRITO: Fine, I won't press that point. But Socrates, be honest with me—is the reason you won't escape because you're worried about us? Are you afraid that if we help you break out, the authorities will punish us—seize our property or worse? If so, don't give it another thought. Saving you is worth any risk we might face. So please, listen to me and agree to escape.

SOCRATES: That is one of my concerns, Crito, yes. But it is by no means the only one.
D: Elegant: Crito suspects Socrates refuses to escape out of concern for his friends' safety and urges him not to worry—they are willing to accept any risk. Socrates acknowledges this is one concern among several, hinting at deeper reasons for staying.
D: Summary: Crito asks whether Socrates is refusing to escape out of fear that his friends will be punished, and assures him they willingly accept the risk. Socrates confirms this is only one of his concerns.
D: Distilled: Crito urges Socrates not to let concern for his friends' safety prevent his escape; Socrates hints at deeper reasons for refusing.
Notes: Speakers: Crito, Socrates. The "informers" (sycophantai) were a notorious feature of Athenian legal culture—professional prosecutors who brought charges for profit. Crito's willingness to risk his fortune reflects his genuine devotion, but Socrates' cryptic reply ("by no means the only one") signals that his refusal is grounded in philosophical principle, not practical fear. This sets up the moral argument that will dominate the rest of the dialogue.

D: Original: CRITO: Fear not—there are persons who are willing to get you out of prison at no great cost; and as for the informers, they are far from being exorbitant in their demands—a little money will satisfy them. My means, which are certainly ample, are at your service, and if you have a scruple about spending all mine, here are strangers who will give you the use of theirs; and one of them, Simmias the Theban, has brought a large sum of money for this very purpose; and Cebes and many others are prepared to spend their money in helping you to escape. I say, therefore, do not hesitate on our account, and do not say, as you did in the court that you will have a difficulty in knowing what to do with yourself anywhere else. For men will love you in other places to which you may go, and not in Athens only; there are friends of mine in Thessaly, if you like to go to them, who will value and protect you, and no Thessalian will give you any trouble. Nor can I think that you are at all justified, Socrates, in betraying your own life when you might be saved; in acting thus you are playing into the hands of your enemies, who are hurrying on your destruction. And further I should say that you are deserting your own children; for you might bring them up and educate them; instead of which you go away and leave them, and they will have to take their chance; and if they do not meet with the usual fate of orphans, there will be small thanks to you. No man should bring children into the world who is unwilling to persevere to the end in their nurture and education.
D: Narrative:
CRITO: Then don't be afraid—there are people ready to get you out of prison, and it won't even cost that much. As for the informers, their demands are quite modest; a little money will satisfy them. My own resources, which are more than enough, are entirely at your disposal. And if you have any hesitation about using all of my money, there are foreigners here who will contribute theirs. One of them, Simmias of Thebes, has brought a large sum specifically for this purpose. Cebes and many others are also prepared to help fund your escape.

So please, don't hold back on our account, and don't repeat what you said at the trial—that you wouldn't know what to do with yourself in exile. People will welcome you in other places, not just Athens. I have friends in Thessaly who will take care of you and protect you, and no one there will give you any trouble.

Beyond all that, Socrates, I don't think you're justified in throwing your life away when you could be saved. You're playing right into the hands of your enemies—doing exactly what they want by hurrying along your own destruction.

And what about your children? You could stay alive to raise and educate them. Instead, you're going to abandon them and leave them to whatever fate befalls orphans. A man who brings children into the world has a responsibility to see their upbringing through to the end.
D: Refashioned:
CRITO: Don't worry about the practical obstacles. There are people willing to arrange your escape, and the cost is manageable. The informers can be bought off cheaply. I have plenty of money, and it's all yours. If that makes you uncomfortable, others are ready to contribute—Simmias of Thebes has brought a large sum for exactly this purpose, and Cebes and many others are prepared to help as well.

So don't hesitate on our account. And don't say, as you did during the trial, that you wouldn't know what to do with yourself outside Athens. You'll be welcomed elsewhere. I have friends in Thessaly who would gladly protect you, and no one there would bother you.

Frankly, Socrates, I think what you're doing is wrong. You're giving up your life when it could be saved. You're handing your enemies exactly what they want. And think of your sons—you could be there to raise and educate them. Instead you'll leave them as orphans to fend for themselves. Anyone who brings children into the world owes it to them to see their upbringing through.
D: Elegant: Crito lays out the practical case for escape: money is available from multiple friends including Simmias and Cebes, exile in Thessaly would be safe and welcoming, and Socrates owes it to his children not to abandon them as orphans. Refusing to escape, Crito argues, is playing directly into the hands of Socrates' enemies.
D: Summary: Crito details the escape plan: funding is available from himself, Simmias, Cebes, and others; Thessaly offers safe refuge; and Socrates has a duty to his children. He argues that choosing death when escape is possible serves only Socrates' enemies.
D: Distilled: Crito presents the practical case for escape: money, safe haven, and the moral obligation to raise one's own children.
Notes: Speaker: Crito. Simmias and Cebes of Thebes are Pythagorean philosophers who appear prominently in the Phaedo as Socrates' principal interlocutors on his final day, debating the immortality of the soul. Crito's mention of Thessaly is ironic—Socrates will later (through the voice of the Laws) describe Thessaly as a lawless place. The argument about children anticipates Socrates' own rebuttal: the Laws will argue that true friends will care for his children whether he lives or dies, and that a just life matters more than mere survival.

D: Original: But you appear to be choosing the easier part, not the better and manlier, which would have been more becoming in one who professes to care for virtue in all his actions, like yourself. And indeed, I am ashamed not only of you, but of us who are your friends, when I reflect that the whole business will be attributed entirely to our want of courage. The trial need never have come on, or might have been managed differently; and this last act, or crowning folly, will seem to have occurred through our negligence and cowardice, who might have saved you, if we had been good for anything; and you might have saved yourself, for there was no difficulty at all. See now, Socrates, how sad and discreditable are the consequences, both to us and you. Make up your mind then, or rather have your mind already made up, for the time of deliberation is over, and there is only one thing to be done, which must be done this very night, and, if we delay at all, will be no longer practicable or possible; I beseech you therefore, Socrates, be persuaded by me, and do as I say.
D: Narrative:
You seem to be choosing the easier path rather than the better and more courageous one—which would be more fitting for someone who claims to have spent his whole life caring about virtue. Honestly, I'm ashamed—not just of you, but of all of us, your friends—when I think about how this whole affair will look. People will say it was entirely due to our cowardice and lack of resolve. The trial itself need never have happened, or could have gone differently. And now this final act of folly—letting you die—will seem like the result of our sheer negligence and spinelessness, since we could have saved you if we were worth anything at all. And you could have saved yourself—there was really no difficulty in it.

Think about it, Socrates—consider how shameful and pitiful the consequences will be, for both you and us. Make your decision now—or rather, your decision should already be made, because the time for deliberation is over. There is only one thing to do, and it must be done tonight. If we delay at all, it will be too late. I beg you, Socrates—listen to me and do as I say.
D: Refashioned:
You're taking the easy way out rather than the brave and honorable path—and that seems beneath someone who has always claimed to value virtue above everything. To be frank, I'm ashamed—not only of you but of all your friends. The whole world will blame our cowardice for everything that happened. The trial didn't have to go the way it did. This final disaster—your death—will look like it happened because we were too cowardly and careless to prevent it, when saving you was well within our power. You could have saved yourself too—it really wouldn't have been hard.

Socrates, think about how disgraceful this will look for all of us. The time for debate is over. There is only one course of action, and it has to happen tonight—if we wait any longer, it will be impossible. Please, Socrates, I'm begging you: listen to me.
D: Elegant: Crito concludes his appeal with a charge of moral cowardice: choosing death over escape is the easy path, unworthy of a man devoted to virtue. The whole affair—trial, conviction, and execution—will be blamed on the friends' spinelessness. The time for deliberation is over; action must come tonight or not at all.
D: Summary: Crito concludes his plea by arguing that Socrates is choosing the easy path over the courageous one, shaming both himself and his friends. He urges immediate action, insisting that tonight is the last chance for escape.
D: Distilled: Crito accuses Socrates of moral cowardice for choosing death over escape and demands an immediate decision.
Notes: Speaker: Crito. This passage is the climax of Crito's appeal and represents the conventional Greek aristocratic view of virtue (arete) as requiring bold, decisive action. Crito's accusation that Socrates is choosing "the easier part, not the better and manlier" ironically inverts Socrates' own framework: Socrates will argue that true courage lies in obeying justice even unto death. The urgency—"it must be done this very night"—adds dramatic tension and sets the stage for Socrates' measured philosophical response in Part 2.

D: Original: SOCRATES: Dear Crito, your zeal is invaluable, if a right one; but if wrong, the greater the zeal the greater the danger; and therefore we ought to consider whether I shall or shall not do as you say. For I am and always have been one of those natures who must be guided by reason, whatever the reason may be which upon reflection appears to me to be the best; and now that this chance has befallen me, I cannot repudiate my own words: the principles which I have hitherto honoured and revered I still honour, and unless we can at once find other and better principles, I am certain not to agree with you; no, not even if the power of the multitude could inflict many more imprisonments, confiscations, deaths, frightening us like children with hobgoblin terrors. What will be the fairest way of considering the question? Shall I return to your old argument about the opinions of men?—we were saying that some of them are to be regarded, and others not. Now were we right in maintaining this before I was condemned? And has the argument which was once good now proved to be talk for the sake of talking—mere childish nonsense? That is what I want to consider with your help, Crito:—whether, under my present circumstances, the argument appears to be in any way different or not; and is to be allowed by me or disallowed. That argument, which, as I believe, is maintained by many persons of authority, was to the effect, as I was saying, that the opinions of some men are to be regarded, and of other men not to be regarded. Now you, Crito, are not going to die to-morrow—at least, there is no human probability of this—and therefore you are disinterested and not liable to be deceived by the circumstances in which you are placed. Tell me then, whether I am right in saying that some opinions, and the opinions of some men only, are to be valued, and that other opinions, and the opinions of other men, are not to be valued. I ask you whether I was right in maintaining this?
D: Narrative:
SOCRATES: Dear Crito, your passion is admirable -- if it is pointed in the right direction. But if it is misguided, the stronger your passion, the greater the danger. So we need to carefully consider whether I should do as you urge or not. For I have always been the kind of person who follows wherever reason leads, guided by whatever argument, upon reflection, seems best to me. And now that this fate has come upon me, I cannot abandon my own principles. The values I have honoured and upheld my whole life I still honour today. Unless we can find better principles to replace them, I will certainly not agree with you -- not even if the power of the crowd could bring upon me many more imprisonments, seizures of property, and executions, trying to frighten us like children with tales of monsters.

What is the fairest way to examine this question? Let me return to your old argument about the opinions of others. We used to say that some opinions deserve our attention and others do not. Were we right to hold this view before my conviction? Or has that argument, which once seemed sound, now turned out to be nothing but empty talk -- mere childish nonsense? That is what I want to examine with your help, Crito: whether, given my present situation, the argument looks any different, or whether it remains the same and should still guide us.

The argument, which I believe many thoughtful people support, was this: that the opinions of some people deserve to be taken seriously, while the opinions of others do not. Now you, Crito, are not about to die tomorrow -- at least, there is no reason to expect it -- and so you can think clearly, without being swayed by the circumstances I find myself in. Tell me, then: was I right in saying that we should value certain opinions, specifically the opinions of certain people, while disregarding others? Was I right to maintain this?
D: Refashioned:
SOCRATES: Dear Crito, your enthusiasm would be wonderful if it is rightly directed. But if it is wrong, the more intense it is, the more dangerous it becomes. So we must think carefully about whether I should follow your advice.

I have always been someone who follows reason. Whatever argument seems strongest after careful thought -- that is what I follow. Now that I face this crisis, I will not throw away my principles. The values I have lived by still stand, and unless we find better ones to replace them, I will not be swayed -- not even if the mob could pile on more punishments, more imprisonments, more threats of death, trying to scare us like children.

So let us think about this fairly. We have always said that some opinions matter and others do not. Was that true before I was sentenced? Or was it just talk? That is what I need to work through with you, Crito -- whether the argument still holds in my current situation.

The principle was this: the opinions of some people are worth considering, while those of others are not. Now, you are not the one facing death tomorrow, so you can think about this without bias. Tell me: was I right to hold this view?
D: Elegant: Socrates insists that his lifelong commitment to reason must not be abandoned under pressure. He asks whether the principle they have always held -- that only some opinions deserve respect, not all -- remains valid even now that he faces execution, or whether fear has changed anything.
D: Summary: Socrates responds to Crito's plea by insisting they must follow reason, not emotion. He reintroduces their longstanding principle that only some opinions are worth heeding, asking Crito whether this still holds true.
D: Distilled: We must follow reason, not fear, and only certain opinions deserve our attention.
Notes: Speaker: Socrates. This passage marks the turning point from Crito's emotional appeal (Part 1) to Socrates' rational counter-argument. Socrates employs his characteristic method of returning to first principles, a hallmark of the Socratic elenchus. The insistence on following reason regardless of consequences echoes the Apology (28b-d), where Socrates declares he will obey the god rather than the court. The phrase about frightening 'like children with hobgoblin terrors' anticipates the Gorgias (473d), where Socrates similarly dismisses threats as irrelevant to philosophical reasoning.

D: Original:
CRITO: Certainly.

SOCRATES: The good are to be regarded, and not the bad?

CRITO: Yes.

SOCRATES: And the opinions of the wise are good, and the opinions of the unwise are evil?

CRITO: Certainly.

SOCRATES: And what was said about another matter? Is the pupil who devotes himself to the practice of gymnastics supposed to attend to the praise and blame and opinion of every man, or of one man only—his physician or trainer, whoever he may be?

CRITO: Of one man only.

SOCRATES: And he ought to fear the censure and welcome the praise of that one only, and not of the many?

CRITO: Clearly so.

SOCRATES: And he ought to act and train, and eat and drink in the way which seems good to his single master who has understanding, rather than according to the opinion of all other men put together?

CRITO: True.

SOCRATES: And if he disobeys and disregards the opinion and approval of the one, and regards the opinion of the many who have no understanding, will he not suffer evil?

CRITO: Certainly he will.

SOCRATES: And what will the evil be, whither tending and what affecting, in the disobedient person?

CRITO: Clearly, affecting the body; that is what is destroyed by the evil.
D: Narrative:
CRITO: Certainly.

SOCRATES: We should value the good opinions and disregard the bad ones?

CRITO: Yes.

SOCRATES: And the opinions of the wise are good, while the opinions of the foolish are bad?

CRITO: Certainly.

SOCRATES: Now, what did we say about the following case? When someone devotes himself to physical training, does he pay attention to the praise, criticism, and opinions of just anyone? Or does he listen only to one person -- his doctor or trainer?

CRITO: Only to that one person.

SOCRATES: So he should welcome the praise and fear the criticism of that one expert alone, not the opinions of the crowd?

CRITO: Clearly so.

SOCRATES: And he should train, exercise, eat, and drink in whatever way his trainer -- the one who truly understands -- thinks best, rather than following the opinions of everyone else combined?

CRITO: True.

SOCRATES: And if he disobeys this expert and disregards his guidance, choosing instead to follow the opinion of the uninformed crowd, will he not suffer harm?

CRITO: He certainly will.

SOCRATES: And what kind of harm will it be? What part of him will it damage?

CRITO: Obviously his body -- that is what gets ruined.
D: Refashioned:
CRITO: Certainly.

SOCRATES: So we should respect good opinions and ignore bad ones?

CRITO: Yes.

SOCRATES: And wise people have good opinions, while foolish people have bad ones?

CRITO: Of course.

SOCRATES: Consider this analogy. An athlete in training -- does he listen to the praise and criticism of every random person, or only to his doctor or coach?

CRITO: Only his coach.

SOCRATES: He should care about the judgment of that one expert, not the crowd?

CRITO: Yes, clearly.

SOCRATES: He should eat, drink, and train according to his coach's guidance -- the person who actually understands -- rather than following the opinions of everyone else put together?

CRITO: True.

SOCRATES: And if he ignores the expert and follows the crowd instead, he will be harmed?

CRITO: Certainly.

SOCRATES: What kind of harm? What part of him suffers?

CRITO: His body, obviously. That is what gets damaged.
D: Elegant: Through the analogy of an athlete who should follow only his trainer's expert guidance rather than the opinions of the uninformed crowd, Socrates establishes that in any domain, we should trust the judgment of the one who knows over the opinions of the many who do not.
D: Summary: Socrates uses the analogy of an athlete's trainer to establish that in matters requiring expertise, we should follow the opinion of the one qualified expert rather than the uninformed majority.
D: Distilled: Just as an athlete should follow only his trainer's guidance, we should heed the expert rather than the crowd.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates, Crito. This passage demonstrates the Socratic method of elenchus at its most structured: Socrates leads Crito through a series of small, undeniable steps toward a larger conclusion. The trainer analogy is one of Socrates' favourite argumentative devices, appearing also in the Gorgias (464b-465d) where he compares genuine expertise to flattery. Crito's brief affirmative responses ('Certainly,' 'Yes,' 'True') show how the elenchus works through securing agreement at each step before advancing to the next.

D: Original: SOCRATES: Very good; and is not this true, Crito, of other things which we need not separately enumerate? In questions of just and unjust, fair and foul, good and evil, which are the subjects of our present consultation, ought we to follow the opinion of the many and to fear them; or the opinion of the one man who has understanding? ought we not to fear and reverence him more than all the rest of the world: and if we desert him shall we not destroy and injure that principle in us which may be assumed to be improved by justice and deteriorated by injustice—there is such a principle?
D: Narrative: SOCRATES: Very good. And is this not equally true of other areas, which we need not list one by one? In matters of justice and injustice, right and wrong, good and evil -- the very questions we are now discussing -- should we follow the opinion of the crowd and fear what they think? Or should we follow the opinion of the one person who truly understands these things? Should we not respect and revere that person's judgment more than the judgment of the entire world? And if we abandon that expert guidance, will we not damage and corrupt the part of us that is strengthened by justice and weakened by injustice? Surely there is such a part of us?
D: Refashioned: SOCRATES: Good. And does this same principle not apply to everything else -- though we need not go through every example? On questions of justice and injustice, right and wrong, good and evil -- the very matters we are debating now -- should we follow the opinion of the crowd, or the opinion of the one person who genuinely understands? Should we not revere that expert's judgment above the opinion of everyone else in the world? If we ignore the expert on these matters, will we not damage the part of ourselves that justice improves and injustice corrupts? There is such a part of us, is there not?
D: Elegant: Socrates extends the trainer analogy to moral questions: just as ignoring expert guidance ruins the body, ignoring the moral expert corrupts the soul -- that part of us improved by justice and degraded by injustice.
D: Summary: Socrates extends the trainer analogy from physical health to moral questions, arguing that in matters of justice and injustice we must follow the expert, or risk corrupting the part of ourselves that is shaped by moral conduct.
D: Distilled: In matters of justice, following the ignorant crowd instead of the moral expert corrupts the soul.
Notes: Speaker: Socrates. This is the pivotal move in the argument: Socrates transfers the trainer analogy from the domain of physical health to the domain of ethics. The unnamed 'part of us' improved by justice and damaged by injustice is the soul (psyche), though Socrates deliberately leaves it unnamed here to draw the identification out of Crito. This argument anticipates the Republic's extended treatment of justice as the health of the soul (Books IV and IX). The rhetorical question format is characteristic of Socratic elenchus, guiding the interlocutor to the desired conclusion.

D: Original:
CRITO: Certainly there is, Socrates.

SOCRATES: Take a parallel instance:—if, acting under the advice of those who have no understanding, we destroy that which is improved by health and is deteriorated by disease, would life be worth having? And that which has been destroyed is—the body?

CRITO: Yes.

SOCRATES: Could we live, having an evil and corrupted body?

CRITO: Certainly not.

SOCRATES: And will life be worth having, if that higher part of man be destroyed, which is improved by justice and depraved by injustice? Do we suppose that principle, whatever it may be in man, which has to do with justice and injustice, to be inferior to the body?

CRITO: Certainly not.

SOCRATES: More honourable than the body?

CRITO: Far more.

SOCRATES: Then, my friend, we must not regard what the many say of us; but what he, the one man who has understanding of just and unjust, will say, and what the truth will say. And therefore you begin in error when you advise that we should regard the opinion of the many about just and unjust, good and evil, honourable and dishonourable.—‘Well,’ someone will say, ‘but the many can kill us.’

CRITO: Yes, Socrates; that will clearly be the answer.

SOCRATES: And it is true; but still I find with surprise that the old argument is unshaken as ever. And I should like to know whether I may say the same of another proposition—that not life, but a good life, is to be chiefly valued?

CRITO: Yes, that also remains unshaken.

SOCRATES: And a good life is equivalent to a just and honourable one—that holds also?

CRITO: Yes, it does.
D: Narrative:
CRITO: There certainly is, Socrates.

SOCRATES: Consider a parallel case. If we followed the advice of those who have no medical knowledge and destroyed the part of us that health improves and disease damages, would life be worth living? And the part that gets destroyed in this case is the body?

CRITO: Yes.

SOCRATES: Could we go on living with a body that is ruined and corrupted?

CRITO: Certainly not.

SOCRATES: Then will life be worth living if that higher part of us is destroyed -- the part that justice improves and injustice corrupts? Or do we think that this principle within us, whatever it is, that has to do with justice and injustice, is less important than the body?

CRITO: Certainly not.

SOCRATES: More valuable than the body?

CRITO: Far more.

SOCRATES: Then, my friend, we must not concern ourselves with what the crowd says about us. We must concern ourselves with what the one person who understands justice and injustice will say -- and with what the truth itself will say. And so your argument starts from a false premise when you urge us to care about what the majority thinks about justice and injustice, good and evil, honour and dishonour. "Well," someone might object, "but the majority can put us to death."

CRITO: Yes, Socrates, that objection will obviously be raised.

SOCRATES: True enough. But I find, to my surprise, that our old argument remains as strong as ever. And I would like to know whether you still agree with another principle as well: that it is not mere life that matters most, but a good life?

CRITO: Yes, that principle still stands.

SOCRATES: And that a good life is the same as a just and honourable life -- does that still hold too?

CRITO: Yes, it does.
D: Refashioned:
CRITO: There certainly is, Socrates.

SOCRATES: Think of it this way. If we took the advice of people with no medical knowledge and wrecked the part of ourselves that health builds up and disease tears down, would life be worth living? We are talking about the body, right?

CRITO: Yes.

SOCRATES: Could we go on living with a body that is completely ruined?

CRITO: No, we could not.

SOCRATES: Then what about the part of us that is even more important than the body -- the part that justice strengthens and injustice destroys? Is life worth living if that is corrupted? Do we really think this principle, whatever it is, that deals with justice and injustice, matters less than the body?

CRITO: Not at all.

SOCRATES: It is more valuable?

CRITO: Far more valuable.

SOCRATES: Then we must stop worrying about what the crowd thinks and focus on what the expert in justice says -- and what the truth says. Your starting point is wrong, Crito, when you tell us to care about majority opinion on matters of right and wrong. Now, someone might say: "But the majority can kill us."

CRITO: Yes, they will certainly say that.

SOCRATES: True. But our old argument still holds firm. And tell me: do we still agree that what matters most is not just living, but living well?

CRITO: Yes.

SOCRATES: And that living well means living justly and honourably?

CRITO: Yes, that holds too.
D: Elegant: Since the soul -- the seat of justice and moral character -- is far more valuable than the body, we cannot afford to corrupt it by following the crowd's opinions any more than we would ruin our health by ignoring a doctor. What matters is not mere survival but living a just and honourable life.
D: Summary: Socrates argues that since the soul is more valuable than the body, corrupting it by following the crowd's moral opinions would make life not worth living. He reaffirms with Crito that a good life means a just and honourable life.
D: Distilled: The soul matters more than the body, so a life corrupted by injustice is not worth living.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates, Crito. This passage completes the expert-opinion argument with two key moves: the a fortiori argument (if life is not worth living with a ruined body, even less so with a ruined soul) and the equation of a good life with a just life. The claim that the soul is 'more honourable' than the body is central to Platonic philosophy, developed extensively in the Phaedo and Republic. The principle that 'not life, but a good life, is to be chiefly valued' echoes the Apology (28b), where Socrates declares that 'the unexamined life is not worth living.' Crito's agreement at each step demonstrates how the elenchus builds toward inescapable conclusions.

D: Original:
SOCRATES: From these premises I proceed to argue the question whether I ought or ought not to try and escape without the consent of the Athenians: and if I am clearly right in escaping, then I will make the attempt; but if not, I will abstain. The other considerations which you mention, of money and loss of character and the duty of educating one’s children, are, I fear, only the doctrines of the multitude, who would be as ready to restore people to life, if they were able, as they are to put them to death—and with as little reason. But now, since the argument has thus far prevailed, the only question which remains to be considered is whether we shall do rightly either in escaping or in suffering others to aid in our escape and paying them in money and thanks, or whether in reality we shall not do rightly; and if the latter, then death or any other calamity which may ensue on my remaining here must not be allowed to enter into the calculation.

CRITO: I think that you are right, Socrates; how then shall we proceed?
D: Narrative:
SOCRATES: From these agreed principles, I will now argue the real question: whether it would be right or wrong for me to try to escape without the permission of the Athenians. If it is clearly right for me to escape, then I will make the attempt. But if it is not right, I will not. The other considerations you raise -- money, reputation, the duty to educate my children -- are, I am afraid, just the concerns of the crowd, the kind of people who would bring the dead back to life as thoughtlessly as they put them to death, if only they had the power to do so.

But since our argument has brought us this far, the only question that remains is this: will we be acting justly if we arrange my escape and pay those who help us? Or will we in fact be acting unjustly? And if we would be acting unjustly, then we must not let the prospect of death or any other consequence enter into our thinking at all.

CRITO: I think you are right, Socrates. How shall we proceed?
D: Refashioned:
SOCRATES: Based on what we have agreed, I now turn to the real question: should I try to escape without the consent of Athens? If escape is clearly the right thing to do, I will go. If not, I will stay.

The other things you mentioned -- money, reputation, raising my children -- those are the concerns of the majority, the same people who would carelessly bring the dead back to life just as readily as they put them to death, if they had the power.

Since our argument has led us here, only one question matters: would escaping be just or unjust? If it would be unjust, then death -- or any other consequence of staying -- must not factor into our decision at all.

CRITO: I think you are right, Socrates. So how do we proceed?
D: Elegant: Having established that only a just life is worth living, Socrates reframes the entire escape question: the only thing that matters is whether escaping would be right or wrong. Consequences like death, money, or reputation are irrelevant if the act itself is unjust.
D: Summary: Socrates applies the agreed principles to the escape question, arguing that the only relevant consideration is whether escaping would be just or unjust, dismissing concerns about money, reputation, and children as the thinking of the crowd.
D: Distilled: The only question that matters is whether escape would be just -- all other consequences are irrelevant.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates, Crito. This passage marks the transition from establishing general principles (the expert argument, the value of the soul) to applying them to the specific question of escape. Socrates systematically dismisses every practical consideration Crito raised in Part 1 -- money, reputation, children -- as 'doctrines of the multitude.' The characterization of the crowd as people who would 'restore people to life as readily as they put them to death' echoes the Gorgias (512a-e), where Socrates argues that preserving life at any cost is not inherently valuable. Crito's concession ('How shall we proceed?') signals his acceptance of Socrates' philosophical framework.

D: Original:
SOCRATES: Let us consider the matter together, and do you either refute me if you can, and I will be convinced; or else cease, my dear friend, from repeating to me that I ought to escape against the wishes of the Athenians: for I highly value your attempts to persuade me to do so, but I may not be persuaded against my own better judgment. And now please to consider my first position, and try how you can best answer me.

CRITO: I will.
D: Narrative:
SOCRATES: Let us think this through together. If you can refute me, do so, and I will be persuaded. But otherwise, my dear friend, please stop repeating that I ought to escape against the wishes of Athens. I value your efforts to convince me -- I truly do -- but I cannot be persuaded to go against my own best judgment. Now please consider my starting point, and try your best to answer me.

CRITO: I will try.
D: Refashioned:
SOCRATES: Let us work through this together. If you can show me I am wrong, I will gladly change my mind. But if you cannot, then please, dear friend, stop urging me to escape against the will of Athens. I deeply appreciate your attempt to persuade me, but I will not act against my own reasoned judgment. Now consider my opening argument, and answer me as best you can.

CRITO: I will.
D: Elegant: Socrates invites Crito to refute his reasoning if he can, but insists he will not act against his own best judgment merely because a friend urges him to. He asks Crito to engage with the argument rather than repeat emotional appeals.
D: Summary: Socrates invites Crito to refute his reasoning if possible, but declares he will not act against his own judgment. He asks Crito to engage with the argument directly.
D: Distilled: Socrates will follow reason, not emotional appeals, and invites Crito to refute him or accept the argument.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates, Crito. This brief exchange establishes the ground rules for the philosophical inquiry that follows. Socrates' insistence on rational persuasion rather than emotional pressure is a defining feature of the Socratic method: the elenchus demands that both parties follow the argument wherever it leads. The phrase 'I may not be persuaded against my own better judgment' recalls the Apology (29d), where Socrates tells the jury he will obey the god rather than them. Crito's willingness to engage ('I will try') shows the cooperative spirit essential to Socratic dialectic.

D: Original: SOCRATES: Are we to say that we are never intentionally to do wrong, or that in one way we ought and in another way we ought not to do wrong, or is doing wrong always evil and dishonourable, as I was just now saying, and as has been already acknowledged by us? Are all our former admissions which were made within a few days to be thrown away? And have we, at our age, been earnestly discoursing with one another all our life long only to discover that we are no better than children? Or, in spite of the opinion of the many, and in spite of consequences whether better or worse, shall we insist on the truth of what was then said, that injustice is always an evil and dishonour to him who acts unjustly? Shall we say so or not?
D: Narrative: SOCRATES: Should we say that we must never intentionally do wrong? Or should we say that sometimes we ought to do wrong and sometimes not? Or is wrongdoing always evil and dishonourable, as I was just saying, and as we have already agreed? Are all the conclusions we reached in the past few days to be thrown away now? Have we, at our age, spent our whole lives in serious philosophical conversation only to discover that we are no better than children? Or should we stand by what we have always held -- regardless of what the majority thinks, and regardless of the consequences -- that injustice is always an evil and a disgrace to the person who commits it? Shall we affirm this, or not?
D: Refashioned:
SOCRATES: Let me put the question directly. Should we say that it is never right to do wrong intentionally? Or that sometimes wrongdoing is acceptable and sometimes not? Or is doing wrong always evil and dishonourable, as we just agreed?

Are we going to throw out everything we have concluded over the course of our lives? Have we, at our age, spent all these years in serious philosophical discussion only to find that we reason no better than children?

Or do we hold firm to what we have always believed -- despite what the crowd thinks, despite whatever consequences may follow -- that injustice is always harmful and shameful to the one who commits it? Do we stand by this, or not?
D: Elegant: Socrates challenges Crito to reaffirm or abandon their lifelong conviction: that wrongdoing is always evil and dishonourable, regardless of circumstances, consequences, or popular opinion. To abandon this principle now would reduce a lifetime of philosophy to childish pretense.
D: Summary: Socrates asks whether they should abandon their lifelong principle that wrongdoing is always evil and dishonourable, or whether they should hold firm regardless of consequences and popular opinion.
D: Distilled: Injustice is always evil and dishonourable -- this principle must not be abandoned under pressure.
Notes: Speaker: Socrates. This passage opens the second chapter of Part 2, shifting from the epistemological argument (whose opinion to trust) to the ethical argument (what justice requires). The rhetorical force of 'have we been no better than children?' pressures Crito to maintain consistency with their shared philosophical commitments. The principle that injustice always harms the doer is a cornerstone of Socratic ethics, argued at length in the Gorgias (469b-479e), where Socrates famously claims it is better to suffer wrong than to do wrong. The appeal to their lifelong philosophical practice connects to the Apology's insistence that 'the unexamined life is not worth living.'

D: Original:
CRITO: Yes.

SOCRATES: Then we must do no wrong?

CRITO: Certainly not.

SOCRATES: Nor, when injured, injure in return, as the many imagine; for we must injure no one at all?

CRITO: Clearly not.

SOCRATES: Again, Crito, may we do evil?

CRITO: Surely not, Socrates.

SOCRATES: And what of doing evil in return for evil, which is the morality of the many—is that just or not?

CRITO: Not just.

SOCRATES: For doing evil to another is the same as injuring him?

CRITO: Very true.
D: Narrative:
CRITO: Yes.

SOCRATES: Then we must never do wrong?

CRITO: Certainly not.

SOCRATES: And when someone wrongs us, we must not wrong them back -- even though most people believe we should. For we must never wrong anyone at all?

CRITO: Clearly not.

SOCRATES: And again, Crito -- may we do evil to another person?

CRITO: Surely not, Socrates.

SOCRATES: And what about repaying evil with evil, which is the common morality of most people -- is that just or not?

CRITO: It is not just.

SOCRATES: Because doing evil to another person is the same as wronging him?

CRITO: Very true.
D: Refashioned:
CRITO: Yes, we should stand by it.

SOCRATES: So we must never do wrong?

CRITO: Never.

SOCRATES: And when someone wrongs us, we must not wrong them in return? Most people think retaliation is justified, but we have said we must never wrong anyone. Correct?

CRITO: Correct.

SOCRATES: And may we do harm to another person?

CRITO: No, Socrates.

SOCRATES: What about returning harm for harm -- the moral code most people live by? Is that just?

CRITO: It is not.

SOCRATES: Because harming someone is the same as wronging them?

CRITO: Exactly.
D: Elegant: Through step-by-step questioning, Socrates secures Crito's agreement to a radical ethical principle: we must never do wrong, never retaliate, and never return evil for evil, because harming another person is itself an act of injustice.
D: Summary: Through a series of rapid questions, Socrates gets Crito to agree that they must never do wrong, never retaliate when wronged, and never return evil for evil, since doing harm to another is itself an injustice.
D: Distilled: We must never do wrong or retaliate, because returning evil for evil is itself unjust.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates, Crito. This rapid-fire elenchus establishes the most radical principle in Socratic ethics: the absolute prohibition on retaliation. This directly contradicts the prevailing Greek moral code, which considered retaliating against enemies a virtue (as seen in Meno 71e and throughout Homer). Socrates explicitly acknowledges this in 2.9. The argument connects closely to the Gorgias (474b-475e), where Socrates argues that doing injustice is worse than suffering it. The identification of 'doing evil' with 'injuring' closes a potential loophole: one cannot claim to be doing justified harm without committing injustice.

D: Original: SOCRATES: Then we ought not to retaliate or render evil for evil to anyone, whatever evil we may have suffered from him. But I would have you consider, Crito, whether you really mean what you are saying. For this opinion has never been held, and never will be held, by any considerable number of persons; and those who are agreed and those who are not agreed upon this point have no common ground, and can only despise one another when they see how widely they differ. Tell me, then, whether you agree with and assent to my first principle, that neither injury nor retaliation nor warding off evil by evil is ever right. And shall that be the premise of our argument? Or do you decline and dissent from this? For so I have ever thought, and continue to think; but, if you are of another opinion, let me hear what you have to say. If, however, you remain of the same mind as formerly, I will proceed to the next step.
D: Narrative:
SOCRATES: Then we ought never to retaliate or return evil for evil to anyone, no matter what we have suffered at their hands. But I want you to think carefully about this, Crito, and make sure you truly mean what you are saying. For this is a view that has never been held, and never will be held, by any large number of people. Those who accept this principle and those who reject it have no common ground between them -- they can only look at each other with contempt when they see how far apart they stand.

So tell me honestly: do you genuinely agree with my starting principle -- that neither doing wrong, nor retaliating, nor defending yourself by returning evil for evil is ever right? Will that serve as the foundation of our argument? Or do you disagree? For this is what I have always believed, and what I continue to believe. But if you hold a different view, say so now. If, however, you stand by what we have agreed, I will move to the next step.
D: Refashioned:
SOCRATES: So we must never retaliate or return evil for evil, no matter what has been done to us. But think carefully about this, Crito -- be sure you really mean it.

This is a position that very few people have ever held, or ever will. Those who believe it and those who reject it stand on completely different ground. They can only look at each other in dismay when they see how deeply they disagree.

So tell me plainly: do you truly accept my foundational principle -- that doing wrong, retaliating, and returning evil for evil are never justified? Will you build the rest of our argument on this? Or do you dissent? This has always been my conviction, and it still is. But if you think differently, tell me now. If you still agree, I will go on to the next point.
D: Elegant: Socrates warns that the principle of never retaliating is held by very few and creates an unbridgeable divide between those who accept it and those who do not. He presses Crito to confirm his genuine commitment before building further arguments upon it.
D: Summary: Socrates emphasizes the radical nature of their agreed principle -- that one must never retaliate -- noting that very few people accept it. He presses Crito to confirm his sincere commitment before proceeding.
D: Distilled: The principle of never returning evil for evil is radical and rarely held, so Crito must be genuinely committed before they can proceed.
Notes: Speaker: Socrates. This passage is remarkable for Socrates' candid acknowledgment that his ethical position is deeply counter-cultural. The observation that those who hold this view and those who reject it 'have no common ground' anticipates modern metaethical debates about moral incommensurability. In the Gorgias (482c-486d), Callicles expresses exactly the contempt Socrates describes, dismissing Socratic ethics as the morality of the weak. Socrates' insistence that Crito confirm his agreement before proceeding reflects the elenchus's requirement of genuine assent -- the argument has force only if both parties honestly endorse each premise.

D: Original:
CRITO: You may proceed, for I have not changed my mind.

SOCRATES: Then I will go on to the next point, which may be put in the form of a question:—Ought a man to do what he admits to be right, or ought he to betray the right?

CRITO: He ought to do what he thinks right.

SOCRATES: But if this is true, what is the application? In leaving the prison against the will of the Athenians, do I wrong any? or rather do I not wrong those whom I ought least to wrong? Do I not desert the principles which were acknowledged by us to be just—what do you say?

CRITO: I cannot tell, Socrates; for I do not know.
D: Narrative:
CRITO: You may proceed, for I have not changed my mind.

SOCRATES: Then I will move to the next point, which I will put as a question: when a person has agreed that something is right, should he follow through and do it, or should he betray what is right?

CRITO: He should do what he believes is right.

SOCRATES: Then consider the implications. If I leave prison against the will of Athens, am I not wronging someone? Or rather, am I not wronging the very people I ought least to wrong? Am I not abandoning the principles we have just agreed are just? What do you say?

CRITO: I cannot answer, Socrates, for I do not know.
D: Refashioned:
CRITO: Go ahead -- I have not changed my mind.

SOCRATES: Then here is the next question: when someone has agreed that a course of action is right, should he carry it out, or should he betray the right?

CRITO: He should do what he thinks is right.

SOCRATES: Then apply this to our situation. If I escape from prison against the wishes of Athens, would I be wronging anyone? More specifically, would I not be wronging the very people I should least of all wrong? Would I not be abandoning the principles of justice we just agreed upon? What is your answer?

CRITO: I cannot tell, Socrates. I do not know.
D: Elegant: Socrates introduces the principle that agreements and commitments must be honoured, then turns it toward the escape question: leaving prison against Athens' will would mean wronging those he should least wrong and betraying his own principles. Crito, sensing where the argument leads, finds himself unable to answer.
D: Summary: Socrates establishes that a person must honour their commitments to do what is right, then asks whether escaping would mean wronging Athens and abandoning their agreed principles of justice. Crito, unable to answer, signals his growing uncertainty.
D: Distilled: One must honour agreements and do what is right; escaping may mean wronging Athens and betraying justice.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates, Crito. This passage is the hinge between Part 2 and Part 3 (the Prosopopoeia of the Laws). Socrates introduces the principle of keeping agreements, which will become the foundation of the Laws' argument about the social contract. Crito's confession of ignorance ('I do not know') is a classic outcome of the elenchus -- the interlocutor reaches aporia (puzzlement), having been led to see that his original position is untenable but not yet grasping the full alternative. This moment of aporia creates the dramatic opening for Part 3, where Socrates will ventriloquize the Laws of Athens to answer the question Crito cannot. The principle that one must 'do what he admits to be right' anticipates the Republic's discussion of justice as each part of the soul and state performing its proper function.

D: Original:
SOCRATES: Then consider the matter in this way:—Imagine that I am about to play truant (you may call the proceeding by any name which you like), and the laws and the government come and interrogate me: ‘Tell us, Socrates,’ they say; ‘what are you about? are you not going by an act of yours to overturn us—the laws, and the whole state, as far as in you lies? Do you imagine that a state can subsist and not be overthrown, in which the decisions of law have no power, but are set aside and trampled upon by individuals?’ What will be our answer, Crito, to these and the like words? Anyone, and especially a rhetorician, will have a good deal to say on behalf of the law which requires a sentence to be carried out. He will argue that this law should not be set aside; and shall we reply, ‘Yes; but the state has injured us and given an unjust sentence.’ Suppose I say that?

CRITO: Very good, Socrates.
D: Narrative:
SOCRATES: Then think about it this way. Imagine that just as I am about to escape from prison -- call it whatever you like -- the Laws and the Government of Athens come and confront me. "Tell us, Socrates," they say, "what exactly do you think you're doing? Aren't you, by this act, attempting to destroy us -- the Laws -- and the entire state, as far as it is in your power? Do you think a state can continue to exist and not be overthrown when the decisions of its courts have no force, but are disregarded and overturned by private individuals?"

What will be our answer, Crito, to these words and others like them? Anyone, and especially a skilled orator, would have a great deal to say in defense of the law that requires a judicial sentence to be carried out. He would argue that this law must not be set aside. Shall we reply, "Yes, but the state has wronged us and passed an unjust sentence"? Suppose I say that?

CRITO: By all means say that, Socrates.
D: Refashioned:
SOCRATES: Consider this thought experiment, Crito. Imagine I am about to escape, and the Laws of Athens themselves appear before me to make their case.

"Socrates," they say, "what are you doing? By escaping, you are trying to destroy us -- the entire legal system -- as far as one person can. Can any state survive when its court decisions are ignored and overturned by individuals acting on their own?"

What would we say to that, Crito? Any good lawyer would argue powerfully that court sentences must be carried out. Should we respond, "But the state wronged us -- the sentence was unjust"?

CRITO: Yes, that is exactly what we should say, Socrates.
D: Elegant: Socrates introduces the famous prosopopoeia: imagine the Laws of Athens themselves confronting him as he tries to escape. They ask whether a state can survive when individuals override its court decisions at will. Socrates anticipates the obvious reply -- that the sentence was unjust -- and asks Crito whether that defense is adequate.
D: Summary: Socrates introduces the dramatic device of the personified Laws of Athens, who ask whether his escape would undermine the entire legal order, and he considers whether claiming the sentence was unjust is a sufficient answer.
D: Distilled: If individuals can override court decisions they dislike, the rule of law collapses entirely.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates, Crito. This passage introduces the prosopopoeia -- the personification of the Laws of Athens -- one of the most celebrated rhetorical devices in ancient philosophy. The technique of giving voice to abstract entities was known in Greek rhetoric, but Plato's use here is uniquely powerful. The Laws' opening question anticipates modern jurisprudential debates about civil disobedience and the rule of law: can a legal system survive selective compliance?

D: Original: SOCRATES: ‘And was that our agreement with you?’ the law would answer; ‘or were you to abide by the sentence of the state?’ And if I were to express my astonishment at their words, the law would probably add: ‘Answer, Socrates, instead of opening your eyes—you are in the habit of asking and answering questions. Tell us,—What complaint have you to make against us which justifies you in attempting to destroy us and the state? In the first place did we not bring you into existence? Your father married your mother by our aid and begat you. Say whether you have any objection to urge against those of us who regulate marriage?’ None, I should reply. ‘Or against those of us who after birth regulate the nurture and education of children, in which you also were trained? Were not the laws, which have the charge of education, right in commanding your father to train you in music and gymnastic?’ Right, I should reply. ‘Well then, since you were brought into the world and nurtured and educated by us, can you deny in the first place that you are our child and slave, as your fathers were before you? And if this is true you are not on equal terms with us; nor can you think that you have a right to do to us what we are doing to you. Would you have any right to strike or revile or do any other evil to your father or your master, if you had one, because you have been struck or reviled by him, or received some other evil at his hands? You would not say this.
D: Narrative:
SOCRATES: "And was that our agreement with you?" the Laws would answer, "or were you to abide by whatever sentence the state imposed?" And if I expressed surprise at their words, the Laws would probably add: "Answer us, Socrates, instead of standing there wide-eyed -- you who are so practiced at asking and answering questions. Tell us: what complaint do you have against us that justifies your attempt to destroy us and the state?

"In the first place, did we not bring you into existence? It was through us that your father married your mother and fathered you. Tell us, do you have any objection to those of us who regulate marriage?" None, I would reply. "Or to those of us who, after birth, oversee the upbringing and education of children -- the same upbringing you received? Were not the laws governing education right to require your father to train you in the arts and in physical fitness?" Right, I would reply.

"Well then, since you were brought into the world and raised and educated by us, can you deny, first of all, that you are our offspring and our servant, as your fathers were before you? And if that is so, you are not on equal terms with us. You cannot claim the right to do to us what we are doing to you. Would you have the right to strike back at your father, or to insult him, or to do him any harm, just because he had struck or insulted you first? You would not claim that right.
D: Refashioned:
SOCRATES: The Laws would respond: "Was that our agreement? Were you not bound to accept the state's judgments?" If I showed surprise, they would press further:

"Answer, Socrates -- you who love questions. What complaint justifies destroying us?

"First: did we not give you life itself? Your father married your mother under our authority and brought you into being. Do you object to our marriage laws?" No, I would say. "Do you object to our laws on education, which required your father to train you in culture and athletics?" No, I would say.

"Then since we brought you into the world, raised you, and educated you, can you deny that you are our child and our subject, as your fathers were? And if so, the relationship is not one of equals. You do not have the right to retaliate against us. You would not claim the right to strike your father back because he struck you first -- would you?"
D: Elegant: The Laws argue that they stand to Socrates as parents stand to a child: they enabled his birth through marriage law, oversaw his education, and shaped him into who he is. Since a child cannot claim equal standing with a parent -- cannot strike back when struck -- neither can a citizen claim equal standing with the laws that made him.
D: Summary: The personified Laws argue that they gave Socrates life, education, and upbringing, establishing a parent-child relationship that places the citizen in an unequal position with no right of retaliation.
D: Distilled: The Laws claim parental authority over citizens: they gave you life and education, so you have no right to destroy them in return.
Notes: Speaker: Socrates (voicing the Laws). The Laws' argument from parental authority is a striking philosophical move: the state is not merely an institution but a quasi-familial entity that precedes and produces the individual. The reference to training in 'music and gymnastic' reflects the standard Athenian education described in the Republic. The inequality argument -- that citizens are not on equal terms with the laws -- has been both influential and controversial, anticipating debates about whether political obligation is truly analogous to filial duty.

D: Original:
And because we think right to destroy you, do you think that you have any right to destroy us in return, and your country as far as in you lies? Will you, O professor of true virtue, pretend that you are justified in this? Has a philosopher like you failed to discover that our country is more to be valued and higher and holier far than mother or father or any ancestor, and more to be regarded in the eyes of the gods and of men of understanding? also to be soothed, and gently and reverently entreated when angry, even more than a father, and either to be persuaded, or if not persuaded, to be obeyed? And when we are punished by her, whether with imprisonment or stripes, the punishment is to be endured in silence; and if she lead us to wounds or death in battle, thither we follow as is right; neither may anyone yield or retreat or leave his rank, but whether in battle or in a court of law, or in any other place, he must do what his city and his country order him; or he must change their view of what is just: and if he may do no violence to his father or mother, much less may he do violence to his country.’ What answer shall we make to this, Crito? Do the laws speak truly, or do they not?

CRITO: I think that they do.
D: Narrative:
"And because we think it right to put you to death, do you think you have any right to destroy us in return -- us and your country, as far as it lies in your power? Will you, the self-proclaimed champion of true virtue, claim to be justified in this? Has a philosopher like you failed to understand that your country is more precious, more exalted, and more sacred than your mother or father or all your ancestors combined -- more worthy of reverence in the eyes of both gods and thoughtful people? That when your country is angry, she must be soothed and gently reasoned with, even more reverently than an angry father? That she must either be persuaded, or else obeyed? And when she punishes us -- whether with imprisonment or beatings -- we must bear it in silence? That if she leads us to battle, to wounds or death, we must follow as duty requires? No one may give way, or retreat, or abandon his post. Whether in war, in a court of law, or anywhere else, a citizen must do what his city and country command -- or else change their minds about what is just through persuasion. And if it is wrong to use violence against your father or mother, how much more wrong is it to use violence against your country?"

What shall we say to this, Crito? Do the Laws speak the truth, or do they not?

CRITO: I think that they do.
D: Refashioned:
