"And because we have decided to put you to death, do you claim the right to destroy us in return? Will you, Socrates -- who profess devotion to virtue -- truly argue that this is just?

"Has a philosopher failed to see that his country is more precious and sacred than mother, father, or any ancestor -- more to be revered in the eyes of gods and wise people? That an angry country must be treated with even more patience than an angry father -- reasoned with gently, and either persuaded or obeyed? That when she punishes you -- with prison or blows -- you must endure it? That when she sends you to war, you must go, even at the cost of wounds or death? That no one may retreat or desert his post?

"In battle, in court, or anywhere else, you must obey your country's commands -- or change her mind through persuasion. If violence against a parent is wrong, violence against your country is far worse."

What do we say, Crito? Are the Laws right?

CRITO: I think they are.
D: Elegant: The Laws elevate the state above even parents in the moral hierarchy: one's country is more sacred than mother or father, and must be either persuaded or obeyed. Violence against the state is the supreme impiety. The citizen's only legitimate options are compliance or persuasion -- never defiance.
D: Summary: The Laws argue that one's country is more sacred than one's parents and must be either persuaded through argument or obeyed -- never resisted through force or evasion -- and Crito agrees.
D: Distilled: Your country stands above your parents in the moral order: you must either persuade her or obey her, never defy her.
Notes: Speaker: Socrates (voicing the Laws), with Crito affirming. This passage presents the strongest version of the Laws' authority claim: the state is holier than parents and ancestors. The 'persuade or obey' doctrine is a crucial qualification -- it is not blind obedience but allows for legitimate dissent through argument, as Socrates himself practiced in the Apology. The military imagery (no retreating from one's post) connects to Socrates' own distinguished service at Potidaea, Amphipolis, and Delium, referenced in the Apology. The passage raises enduring questions about the limits of political obligation.

D: Original: SOCRATES: Then the laws will say: ‘Consider, Socrates, if we are speaking truly that in your present attempt you are going to do us an injury. For, having brought you into the world, and nurtured and educated you, and given you and every other citizen a share in every good which we had to give, we further proclaim to any Athenian by the liberty which we allow him, that if he does not like us when he has become of age and has seen the ways of the city, and made our acquaintance, he may go where he pleases and take his goods with him. None of us laws will forbid him or interfere with him. Anyone who does not like us and the city, and who wants to emigrate to a colony or to any other city, may go where he likes, retaining his property. But he who has experience of the manner in which we order justice and administer the state, and still remains, has entered into an implied contract that he will do as we command him. And he who disobeys us is, as we maintain, thrice wrong; first, because in disobeying us he is disobeying his parents; secondly, because we are the authors of his education; thirdly, because he has made an agreement with us that he will duly obey our commands; and he neither obeys them nor convinces us that our commands are unjust; and we do not rudely impose them, but give him the alternative of obeying or convincing us;—that is what we offer, and he does neither. These are the sort of accusations to which, as we were saying, you, Socrates, will be exposed if you accomplish your intentions; you, above all other Athenians.’
D: Narrative:
SOCRATES: Then the Laws will say: "Consider, Socrates, whether we are telling the truth when we say that you are now attempting to wrong us. For after bringing you into the world, raising you, educating you, and giving you and every other citizen a share in all the good things we had to offer, we further made a public declaration. We announced that any Athenian, once he had come of age and observed how the city operates and how we -- the Laws -- function, was free to leave if he was not satisfied. He could take his property and go wherever he wished -- to a colony or to any foreign city. None of us Laws would stand in his way or forbid it.

"But whoever remains, after seeing how we administer justice and govern the state, has by that act entered into an implied agreement to obey our commands. And anyone who disobeys us is, we maintain, guilty of three wrongs: first, because he is disobeying his parents; second, because he is defying the very ones who educated him; and third, because after agreeing to obey us, he neither obeys nor persuades us that our commands are wrong. We do not issue harsh ultimatums. We give every citizen two options: obey, or convince us we are wrong. He does neither.

"These are the charges to which you, Socrates, will be exposed if you carry out your plan -- you, above all other Athenians."
D: Refashioned:
SOCRATES: The Laws continue their argument:

"We brought you into the world, raised you, educated you, and gave every citizen a share of our benefits. Beyond that, we made an open offer: any Athenian who comes of age, sees how the city works, and is dissatisfied is free to leave with all his property. No law will stop him.

"But whoever stays -- having seen our system of justice and governance -- has entered into an implied contract to follow our rules. Anyone who then disobeys is guilty on three counts: first, he defies his parents; second, he defies his educators; third, he breaks an agreement he freely made. And we do not force blind obedience -- we offer a choice: obey our commands, or persuade us they are wrong. The disobedient citizen does neither.

"You, Socrates, above all Athenians, will face these charges if you escape."
D: Elegant: The Laws introduce a social contract argument: Athens allows any dissatisfied citizen to emigrate freely, so those who stay have implicitly agreed to obey. Disobedience wrongs the state as parent, as educator, and as a contractual partner -- especially since the state offers the option of persuasion rather than demanding blind compliance.
D: Summary: The Laws argue that Athens permits free emigration, so any citizen who remains has entered an implied contract to obey; disobedience constitutes a triple wrong against parents, educators, and a freely made agreement.
D: Distilled: By choosing to stay in Athens when free to leave, you implicitly agreed to abide by its laws -- an agreement you now propose to break.
Notes: Speaker: Socrates (voicing the Laws). This passage contains one of the earliest formulations of social contract theory in Western philosophy, anticipating by two millennia the arguments of Hobbes, Locke, and Rousseau. The 'implied contract' -- consent through continued residence when emigration is available -- became a foundational concept in political philosophy. The 'persuade or obey' qualification is significant: the Laws do not demand unconditional submission but offer a deliberative alternative, reflecting Athenian democratic ideals. The phrase 'you above all other Athenians' sets up the personal argument that follows in 3.5.

D: Original:
Suppose now I ask, why I rather than anybody else? they will justly retort upon me that I above all other men have acknowledged the agreement. ‘There is clear proof,’ they will say, ‘Socrates, that we and the city were not displeasing to you. Of all Athenians you have been the most constant resident in the city, which, as you never leave, you may be supposed to love. For you never went out of the city either to see the games, except once when you went to the Isthmus, or to any other place unless when you were on military service; nor did you travel as other men do. Nor had you any curiosity to know other states or their laws: your affections did not go beyond us and our state; we were your especial favourites, and you acquiesced in our government of you; and here in this city you begat your children, which is a proof of your satisfaction. Moreover, you might in the course of the trial, if you had liked, have fixed the penalty at banishment; the state which refuses to let you go now would have let you go then. But you pretended that you preferred death to exile, and that you were not unwilling to die. And now you have forgotten these fine sentiments, and pay no respect to us the laws, of whom you are the destroyer; and are doing what only a miserable slave would do, running away and turning your back upon the compacts and agreements which you made as a citizen. And first of all answer this very question: Are we right in saying that you agreed to be governed according to us in deed, and not in word only? Is that true or not?’ How shall we answer, Crito? Must we not assent?

CRITO: We cannot help it, Socrates.
D: Narrative:
Suppose I then ask, "Why me rather than anyone else?" They will rightly point out that I, above all people, have acknowledged this agreement. "The proof is clear, Socrates," they will say, "that we and the city were not displeasing to you. Of all Athenians, you have been the most devoted resident of this city. You never left it, which can only mean you loved it. You never traveled to see the games -- except once, to the Isthmian Games -- and you never went anywhere else unless it was for military service. You had no curiosity about other states or their laws. Your loyalties never extended beyond us and our city. We were your special choice, and you consented to live under our governance. You even raised your children here -- proof of how content you were.

"Furthermore, during your trial you could have proposed exile as your penalty if you had wished. The state that now refuses to let you go would have allowed it then. But you made a great show of preferring death to exile, declaring that you were not afraid to die. And now you have abandoned those noble words and show no respect for us, the Laws, whom you are trying to destroy. You are behaving like the most wretched slave -- running away and breaking the agreements you made as a citizen.

"So answer this question first: are we right in saying that you agreed to live under our governance not just in word, but in deed? Is that true, or is it not?"

How shall we answer, Crito? Must we not agree?

CRITO: We have no choice, Socrates.
D: Refashioned:
If I ask, "Why single me out?" the Laws will answer:

"The evidence against you is overwhelming, Socrates. Of all Athenians, you were the most constant resident. You never left the city except for military service and once for the Isthmian Games. You never traveled, never explored other states or their laws. Your entire life was lived within our walls. You chose us above all others and raised your children here.

"More than that: at your trial, you could have proposed exile instead of death. Athens would have accepted. But you declared that you preferred death to exile and claimed you were unafraid to die. Now you abandon those fine words. You disrespect us -- the very Laws you claim to honor -- and behave like a runaway slave, breaking every agreement you made as a citizen.

"Answer truthfully: did you agree to live under our governance in deed, not merely in word?"

What can we say, Crito?

CRITO: We cannot deny it, Socrates.
D: Elegant: The Laws make the case personal: Socrates, of all Athenians, has demonstrated the deepest commitment to the city. He never left, never explored other states, raised his children in Athens, and at trial chose death over exile. For him to now flee would be the most hypocritical betrayal of a lifetime's freely demonstrated allegiance.
D: Summary: The Laws argue that Socrates specifically has shown lifelong devotion to Athens by never leaving the city, and that at his trial he explicitly chose death over exile -- making his proposed escape uniquely hypocritical.
D: Distilled: Socrates' entire life -- his constant residence, his refusal of exile at trial -- constitutes an irrefutable demonstration of consent to Athens' laws.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates (voicing the Laws), Crito. This passage contains crucial historical details: Socrates' famous reluctance to leave Athens, his single trip to the Isthmian Games, and his military service (at Potidaea, Amphipolis, and Delium, referenced in the Apology 28e). The reference to proposing exile at trial directly connects to Apology 37c-e, where Socrates rejected exile as meaningless, arguing that other cities would treat him no better. The 'runaway slave' comparison is pointed: in Athenian law, slaves who fled broke their owner's authority, just as Socrates would break the Laws' authority. The argument that voluntary residence constitutes consent became central to Locke's theory of tacit consent in the Second Treatise.

D: Original: SOCRATES: Then will they not say: ‘You, Socrates, are breaking the covenants and agreements which you made with us at your leisure, not in any haste or under any compulsion or deception, but after you have had seventy years to think of them, during which time you were at liberty to leave the city, if we were not to your mind, or if our covenants appeared to you to be unfair. You had your choice, and might have gone either to Lacedaemon or Crete, both which states are often praised by you for their good government, or to some other Hellenic or foreign state. Whereas you, above all other Athenians, seemed to be so fond of the state, or, in other words, of us, her laws (and who would care about a state which has no laws?), that you never stirred out of her; the halt, the blind, the maimed were not more stationary in her than you were. And now you run away and forsake your agreements. Not so, Socrates, if you will take our advice; do not make yourself ridiculous by escaping out of the city.
D: Narrative:
SOCRATES: Then will they not say: "You, Socrates, are breaking the agreements and commitments you made with us -- not in haste, not under compulsion, not because you were deceived, but after you had seventy years to reflect on them. During all that time, you were free to leave the city if we did not satisfy you, or if our terms seemed unfair. You had every opportunity to go elsewhere. You could have gone to Sparta or Crete, whose systems of government you so often praised. Or you could have gone to any other Greek or foreign state.

"But you, more than any other Athenian, seemed devoted to this city -- or rather, to us, the Laws (for who would love a city without laws?). You never stirred from Athens. The lame, the blind, the disabled were no more rooted to this place than you were.

"And now you would run away and abandon your agreements? Do not do this, Socrates. If you take our advice, do not make yourself ridiculous by escaping from the city."
D: Refashioned:
SOCRATES: The Laws would continue:

"You are breaking agreements you made freely -- not in a moment of panic, not under pressure, not through deception, but after seventy years of reflection. You were always free to leave if our terms seemed unfair. You could have moved to Sparta or Crete, whose governments you praised so often, or to any other state.

"But you, Socrates, clung to this city more than any other Athenian -- more than the lame or blind who physically could not leave. You loved us, the Laws, above all.

"And now, after all that, you would run away? Take our advice: do not make yourself ridiculous by fleeing."
D: Elegant: The Laws emphasize the duration and freedom of Socrates' commitment: seventy years of voluntary residence, free to leave at any time, praising other cities' laws yet never moving to them. Breaking such a long and freely sustained agreement would be not only wrong but absurd.
D: Summary: The Laws stress that Socrates had seventy years to leave Athens if dissatisfied, yet stayed more devotedly than any other citizen, making his proposed escape a ridiculous betrayal of freely chosen commitments.
D: Distilled: After seventy years of voluntary residence in Athens -- always free to leave -- breaking your agreement now would be both unjust and absurd.
Notes: Speaker: Socrates (voicing the Laws). The reference to Sparta (Lacedaemon) and Crete reflects Socrates' well-known admiration for their constitutions, discussed in the Republic and Laws. The seventy years mentioned correspond to Socrates' age at the time of his trial (he was born c. 470 BCE and tried in 399 BCE). The Laws' argument from duration strengthens the social contract: this was not a hasty or uninformed choice but a lifelong commitment. The vivid comparison to the lame and blind underscores how extraordinary Socrates' attachment to Athens was -- a devotion now used against him.

D: Original: ‘For just consider, if you transgress and err in this sort of way, what good will you do either to yourself or to your friends? That your friends will be driven into exile and deprived of citizenship, or will lose their property, is tolerably certain; and you yourself, if you fly to one of the neighbouring cities, as, for example, Thebes or Megara, both of which are well governed, will come to them as an enemy, Socrates, and their government will be against you, and all patriotic citizens will cast an evil eye upon you as a subverter of the laws, and you will confirm in the minds of the judges the justice of their own condemnation of you. For he who is a corrupter of the laws is more than likely to be a corrupter of the young and foolish portion of mankind. Will you then flee from well-ordered cities and virtuous men? and is existence worth having on these terms? Or will you go to them without shame, and talk to them, Socrates? And what will you say to them? What you say here about virtue and justice and institutions and laws being the best things among men? Would that be decent of you? Surely not.
D: Narrative:
"For think carefully: if you break the law in this way, what good will you do yourself or your friends? It is practically certain that your friends will be driven into exile, stripped of their citizenship, or lose their property. And you yourself, if you flee to one of the nearby cities -- Thebes or Megara, for example, both of which are well governed -- will arrive as an enemy, Socrates. Their governments will be set against you, and every patriotic citizen will look on you with suspicion as someone who undermines the rule of law. You will only confirm in the minds of the jurors who condemned you that their verdict was just. For anyone who corrupts the laws is very likely to be seen as a corrupter of the young and impressionable as well.

"Will you then avoid well-governed cities and virtuous people? And if you do, is life even worth living on those terms? Or will you approach such people without shame and try to talk to them, Socrates? And what will you say? The same things you said here -- that virtue, justice, institutions, and laws are the most important things in human life? Don't you think that would look disgraceful? It surely would."
D: Refashioned:
"Think about what actually happens if you escape. Your friends will almost certainly face exile, loss of citizenship, or seizure of property.

"And you? If you flee to a well-governed city like Thebes or Megara, you will arrive as an enemy. Their governments will oppose you, and every law-abiding citizen will view you as a lawbreaker. You will only prove your Athenian judges right -- if someone undermines the law, he is likely a corrupter of the young too.

"So will you avoid all well-governed cities? If so, what kind of life is that? Or will you go to them shamelessly and lecture them about virtue, justice, and the importance of law -- while being living proof that you abandoned all three? How could that be anything but disgraceful?"
D: Elegant: The Laws argue that escape is practically self-defeating: well-governed cities will reject Socrates as a lawbreaker, confirming his conviction, and he could never again credibly speak about justice and virtue -- the very things that defined his life's work.
D: Summary: The Laws warn that fleeing to well-governed cities like Thebes or Megara would make Socrates an enemy there, confirm his conviction, and destroy his credibility to speak about virtue and justice.
D: Distilled: Escaping would prove your enemies right and make it impossible to advocate for justice and virtue anywhere again.
Notes: Speaker: Socrates (voicing the Laws). Thebes and Megara were prominent city-states near Athens; Thebes was home to Socrates' friends Simmias and Cebes (who appear in the Phaedo). The argument cleverly turns Socrates' own values against the escape plan: a man who spent his life championing law and virtue cannot credibly do so after becoming a fugitive. The phrase 'corrupter of the young' directly echoes the charges at Socrates' trial (Apology 24b-c), suggesting that escape would retrospectively validate the prosecution's case.

D: Original: But if you go away from well-governed states to Crito’s friends in Thessaly, where there is great disorder and licence, they will be charmed to hear the tale of your escape from prison, set off with ludicrous particulars of the manner in which you were wrapped in a goatskin or some other disguise, and metamorphosed as the manner is of runaways; but will there be no one to remind you that in your old age you were not ashamed to violate the most sacred laws from a miserable desire of a little more life? Perhaps not, if you keep them in a good temper; but if they are out of temper you will hear many degrading things; you will live, but how?—as the flatterer of all men, and the servant of all men; and doing what?—eating and drinking in Thessaly, having gone abroad in order that you may get a dinner. And where will be your fine sentiments about justice and virtue? Say that you wish to live for the sake of your children—you want to bring them up and educate them—will you take them into Thessaly and deprive them of Athenian citizenship? Is this the benefit which you will confer upon them? Or are you under the impression that they will be better cared for and educated here if you are still alive, although absent from them; for your friends will take care of them? Do you fancy that if you are an inhabitant of Thessaly they will take care of them, and if you are an inhabitant of the other world that they will not take care of them? Nay; but if they who call themselves friends are good for anything, they will—to be sure they will.
D: Narrative:
"But suppose you leave well-governed states behind and go instead to Crito's friends in Thessaly, where there is widespread disorder and lawlessness. No doubt they will be delighted to hear the tale of your escape from prison, complete with amusing details about how you were disguised in a goatskin or some other costume, made up like a common fugitive. But will no one point out that in your old age you were not ashamed to cling to life by violating the most sacred laws? Perhaps not -- if you keep everyone in a good mood. But if they turn on you, you will hear many humiliating things.

"And how will you live? As a flatterer and a servant to everyone, doing whatever it takes to stay in their good graces. And doing what exactly? Eating and drinking in Thessaly, as if you went abroad just to get a fine meal. And where will your noble speeches about justice and virtue have gone?

"Or say you want to live for the sake of your children -- to raise and educate them. Will you take them to Thessaly and deprive them of Athenian citizenship? Is that the great favor you plan to do them? Or do you imagine that they will be better raised and educated with you alive but in exile than they would be without you? Your friends will look after them. Will they care for your children if you are in Thessaly but neglect them if you are in the underworld? If those who call themselves your friends are worth anything at all, they will care for them regardless. Surely they will."
D: Refashioned:
"Or suppose you go to Thessaly, to Crito's friends, where there is disorder and lawlessness. They will enjoy the story of your prison escape -- the disguise, the goatskin costume, all the ridiculous details. But someone will eventually ask: in your old age, were you not ashamed to violate the most sacred laws just for a little more life?

"Keep your hosts happy and they might tolerate you. Offend them and you will hear degrading things. You will live as a flatterer, a servant to everyone. You will have gone abroad for nothing more than dinner. And your great principles about justice and virtue? Gone.

"As for your children: will you drag them to Thessaly and strip them of their Athenian citizenship? Is that a favor? And if you leave them behind, will your exile help them? Your friends will care for them whether you are in Thessaly or in the next world. If your friends are any good at all, they will look after your children regardless."
D: Elegant: If Socrates flees to lawless Thessaly, he will live as a flatterer in disgrace, his philosophical principles abandoned. His children will be better served by loyal friends in Athens than by a fugitive father in exile -- and those friends will care for them whether he is alive or dead.
D: Summary: The Laws argue that life in Thessaly would be degrading -- living as a flatterer, abandoning his principles -- and that his children would be no better off with a fugitive father, since loyal friends will care for them regardless.
D: Distilled: Exile in Thessaly means living as a flatterer without principles, while your children will be cared for by friends whether you are alive or dead.
Notes: Speaker: Socrates (voicing the Laws). Thessaly was known for political instability and was considered culturally inferior to Athens -- the Laws' disdain is pointed. The goatskin disguise detail suggests a concrete escape plan may have been proposed. The argument about children directly answers Crito's appeal in 1.6, where he argued Socrates owed it to his children to escape. The Laws invert this: true care for children means maintaining principles, not clinging to life at any cost. Simmias and Cebes, mentioned earlier as willing to fund the escape, would later be present at Socrates' death in the Phaedo.

D: Original: ‘Listen, then, Socrates, to us who have brought you up. Think not of life and children first, and of justice afterwards, but of justice first, that you may be justified before the princes of the world below. For neither will you nor any that belong to you be happier or holier or juster in this life, or happier in another, if you do as Crito bids. Now you depart in innocence, a sufferer and not a doer of evil; a victim, not of the laws, but of men. But if you go forth, returning evil for evil, and injury for injury, breaking the covenants and agreements which you have made with us, and wronging those whom you ought least of all to wrong, that is to say, yourself, your friends, your country, and us, we shall be angry with you while you live, and our brethren, the laws in the world below, will receive you as an enemy; for they will know that you have done your best to destroy us. Listen, then, to us and not to Crito.’
D: Narrative:
"Listen, then, Socrates, to us who raised you. Do not put life and children before justice. Put justice first, so that when you stand before the rulers of the underworld, you may have a defense.

"For if you do as Crito urges, neither you nor anyone close to you will be happier, more virtuous, or more just in this life -- nor happier in the next.

"As things stand now, you depart as an innocent man -- a victim, not a perpetrator of evil; wronged not by the Laws but by other men. But if you escape, returning evil for evil, injury for injury, breaking the agreements you made with us, and wronging those you should least of all wrong -- yourself, your friends, your country, and us -- then we shall be angry with you while you live. And our brothers, the Laws of the underworld, will not receive you kindly, for they will know that you did your best to destroy us.

"Listen to us, then, and not to Crito."
D: Refashioned:
"Hear us, Socrates -- we who brought you up. Do not put life and children ahead of justice. Put justice first, so you can face the judges of the afterlife with a clear conscience.

"If you follow Crito's advice, no one -- not you, not your loved ones -- will be happier, holier, or more just, in this life or the next.

"Right now, you leave as an innocent man. You are a victim, not a wrongdoer -- harmed not by the Laws but by men. But if you escape -- returning evil for evil, breaking your agreements, wronging yourself, your friends, your country, and us -- we will oppose you while you live, and the Laws of the underworld will reject you when you die.

"Listen to us. Not to Crito."
D: Elegant: The Laws deliver their final appeal: put justice above life and children. As things stand, Socrates dies innocent -- a victim of men, not of law. But if he escapes, he becomes the wrongdoer, and both the Laws of Athens and the Laws of the underworld will condemn him.
D: Summary: The Laws deliver their concluding exhortation: Socrates should prioritize justice over survival, departing innocent as a victim of men rather than becoming a lawbreaker who will be condemned by both the living Laws and those of the afterlife.
D: Distilled: Die innocent as a victim of injustice, or live guilty as a destroyer of law -- the Laws demand Socrates choose justice over survival.
Notes: Speaker: Socrates (voicing the Laws). This is the Laws' peroration -- their final, most emotionally powerful appeal. The reference to 'the Laws of the world below' introduces a theological dimension: justice transcends the earthly realm. The phrase 'returning evil for evil' connects to the ethical principle established in 2.8-2.9, completing the dialogue's argumentative arc. The contrast between departing innocent versus departing guilty is the culmination of the entire prosopopoeia. The Phaedo will continue this theme, with Socrates facing death serenely, confident in the justice of his choice.

D: Original:
This, dear Crito, is the voice which I seem to hear murmuring in my ears, like the sound of the flute in the ears of the mystic; that voice, I say, is humming in my ears, and prevents me from hearing any other. And I know that anything more which you may say will be vain. Yet speak, if you have anything to say.

CRITO: I have nothing to say, Socrates.

SOCRATES: Leave me then, Crito, to fulfil the will of God, and to follow whither he leads.
D: Narrative:
SOCRATES: This, dear Crito, is the voice I seem to hear ringing in my ears, like the sound of a flute in the ears of a mystic. That voice, I tell you, is reverberating inside me and makes it impossible for me to hear anything else. And I know that whatever more you might say will be in vain. Yet speak, if you have anything to say.

CRITO: I have nothing to say, Socrates.

SOCRATES: Then let it be, Crito. Let us follow this path, since this is where God leads us.
D: Refashioned:
SOCRATES: This, dear Crito, is the voice I hear echoing in my mind, like the sound of sacred flutes drowning out everything else. I know that anything more you say will be futile. But speak if you wish.

CRITO: I have nothing to say, Socrates.

SOCRATES: Then let it be. Let us follow this course, for this is where God leads.
D: Elegant: Socrates declares that the voice of the Laws rings in his ears like sacred music, drowning out all other arguments. Crito, who arrived at dawn desperate to save his friend, concedes that he has nothing more to say. Socrates accepts his fate as divinely guided.
D: Summary: Socrates says the voice of the Laws overwhelms all other arguments; Crito concedes he has nothing more to say; and Socrates resolves to accept his fate, following where God leads.
D: Distilled: The voice of the Laws drowns out all objections, and Socrates accepts his death as the path of justice and divine will.
Notes: Speakers: Socrates, Crito. The dialogue ends with a striking image: the Laws' voice as mystical flute music that overwhelms all other sound, evoking the Corybantic rites (ecstatic religious ceremonies). This mystical framing transforms a legal and philosophical argument into something approaching religious conviction. Crito's silence -- 'I have nothing to say' -- mirrors his opening urgency and marks his complete persuasion. Socrates' final words, 'where God leads,' connect to the Apology's theme of divine mission and anticipate the Phaedo, where Socrates will drink the hemlock with extraordinary calm. The Crito thus bridges the Apology (trial and conviction) and the Phaedo (death), completing the trilogy of Socrates' final days.
